coredata.dc:title: Sunlight-assisted photocatalytic degradation of azo-dye using zinc-sulfide embedded reduced graphene oxide

coredata.dc:description: Herein, Zinc sulfide embedded reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites (ZnS-rGO) have been prepared using a simple precipitation method. The as-prepared ZnS-rGO has been applied for sunlight-promoted selective degradation of Congo Red (CR), an azo dye in a short time and even when combined with spiking industrial wastewater. The ZnS-rGO shows ~98% degradation of CR dye with a rate constant of 0.0266 min−1 by following pseudo-first-order kinetics. Sunlight as a sustainable energy source produces the best results (~three times higher) than experiments done in artificial bulb light, during photocatalytic degradation of similar concentrations of CR dye. The effect of dye concentration and loading of catalyst on photocatalytic degradation efficiency has also been explored. The role of reactive oxygen species responsible for photocatalytic degradation is examined by employing trap experiments, confirms the significant contribution of superoxide radicals. Additionally, ZnS-rGO is able to degrade CR dye in a broad pH range and used to degrade CR from industrially spiked wastewater samples to demonstrate the photocatalyst's potential for their use in the real-world applications. Moreover, comparative NMR and FTIR analysis support the photocatalytic degradation of CR dye.

head.ce:title: Sunlight-assisted photocatalytic degradation of azo-dye using zinc-sulfide embedded reduced graphene oxide

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, Zinc sulfide embedded reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites (ZnS-rGO) have been prepared using a simple precipitation method. The as-prepared ZnS-rGO has been applied for sunlight-promoted selective degradation of Congo Red (CR), an azo dye in a short time and even when combined with spiking industrial wastewater. The ZnS-rGO shows ~98% degradation of CR dye with a rate constant of 0.0266 min−1 by following pseudo-first-order kinetics. Sunlight as a sustainable energy source produces the best results (~three times higher) than experiments done in artificial bulb light, during photocatalytic degradation of similar concentrations of CR dye. The effect of dye concentration and loading of catalyst on photocatalytic degradation efficiency has also been explored. The role of reactive oxygen species responsible for photocatalytic degradation is examined by employing trap experiments, confirms the significant contribution of superoxide radicals. Additionally, ZnS-rGO is able to degrade CR dye in a broad pH range and used to degrade CR from industrially spiked wastewater samples to demonstrate the photocatalyst's potential for their use in the real-world applications. Moreover, comparative NMR and FTIR analysis support the photocatalytic degradation of CR dye.

ce:section.ce:para: Presently, water soluble contaminants (organic dyes, toxic metal ions, pesticides, antibiotics etc) (Geissen et al., 2015; Hiew et al., 2018; Mitra et al., 2019) raises a serious concern for human and environmental health (Hassaan et al., 2017; Lellis et al., 2019; Sankhla et al., 2016). From the many known water contaminants, synthetic organic dyes majorly used for coloring purposes hold an impressive position (Alderete et al., 2021). Among the numerous dyes, the much more frequently utilized dyes are from the group of azo compounds, which are utilized in a variety of goods, including inks, textiles, paints, food, home goods, and cosmetics (Benkhaya et al., 2020; do Vale-Júnior et al., 2018). A large amount of azo dye, specifically Congo Red (CR), has been utilized in industry of textile, and whenever this dye is employed in textile sector, some of it cannot connect with fibers and instead stays in bulk solution (Raval et al., 2016). Out of the various azo dyes, CR dye is made of benzidine, is widely known for being carcinogenic effects and for being very much difficult to decompose or remove from waste effluents (Raval et al., 2016). Thus, the establishment of innovative methods for the degradation of CR dye from wastewater is therefore in high demand.

A few popular methods for getting rid of azo dyes include adsorption (Li et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020), flocculation (Lau et al., 2014; Quinlan et al., 2015), biodegradation (Abd El-Rahim et al., 2021; Kashefi et al., 2019), ultrafiltration (Fradj et al., 2020; Lykidou et al., 2016), etc. However, the production of secondary effluents is a significant issue that requires careful attention (Chen et al., 2017). The widely recommended prevention methods include chemical oxidation and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) (Muniyasamy et al., 2020; Navarro et al., 2017). However, it showed some limitations based on cost. For the same, the process of photocatalysts can be used, which are quite capable of breaking down the targeted dye molecule by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) in presence of light (Bhati et al., 2019; Garg et al., 2022b; Sadeghpour et al., 2022; Singh et al., 2019). This photocatalysis process has been simply drawn by using different light sources that mostly contain UV, visible and sunlight (Bhati et al., 2018a; Gupta and Pandey, 2019; Khalilian et al., 2015; Magdalane et al., 2017; Singh et al., 2017). Among them, renewable sunlight is one of the best source of energy (Aggarwal et al., 2020) that needs to be explored well in the field of photocatalysis.

For the same, different types of composite material based on graphene oxide (GO) (Das et al., 2022; Das et al., 2021; Yaqoob et al., 2020), aerogel (Kaushik et al., 2022a), reduced graphene oxide (rGO) (Miao et al., 2016; Mitra et al., 2019; Rahnama et al., 2021), carbon nanotube (Hemraj-Benny et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2022; Yibeltal et al., 2020), zinc oxide (ZnO) (Boutra et al., 2020; Lonkar et al., 2018; Pang et al., 2018), polymer film (Alshabanat and AL-Anazy, 2018) have been explored for the breakdown of CR dye. Among them, composite materials based on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) are regarded as the important material towards optoelectronic applications due to their outstanding light induced charge production in both the solid and solution phases (Ibrahim et al., 2018). In composites of rGO, photo-induced electrons move from the associated optical materials' conduction band to the rGO sheets, which reduce the chances of electron-hole recombination and enhances the concentration of photogenerated charge carriers (Borthakur and Das, 2018).

The performance of the metal oxide semiconductors-based rGO composite is severely hampered by oxygen deficiencies and interfacial absorption–desorption processes that lead to photocurrent destabilization (Ibrahim et al., 2017; Sung et al., 2021). To overcome this restriction for optoelectronic applications, scientists have designed rGO composites with metal chalcogenide semiconductor (Ibrahim et al., 2018). For this purpose, semiconductor zinc sulfide (ZnS) has demonstrated excellent thermal stability, exceptional transport characteristics, and good electrical mobility due to having wide band gap. Due to high negative reduction potential of excited electrons and quick generation of excitons in presence of light, ZnS is a prospective material for photocatalytic and optoelectronic applications. (Ibrahim et al., 2017) ZnS-rGO nanocomposite has been employed for a number of photocatalytic uses, namely the reduction of p-nitrophenol/Cr(VI) (Azimi et al., 2016; Ibrahim et al., 2017), dye degradation (Mahvelati-Shamsabadi et al., 2018; Miao et al., 2016; Mitra et al., 2019), water oxidation (Mahvelati-Shamsabadi and Goharshadi, 2018), and the development of lithium-ion batteries(Feng et al., 2016) and photovoltaic devices (Qin et al., 2017). But application of ZnS-rGO for the sunlight-assisted azo dyes degradation, has not been done yet.

In the current study, we have developed ZnS embedded rGO by following simple precipitation method and employed for sunlight-assisted degradation of azo dyes specifically CR (60 mg/L) from the numerous other organic dye that were evaluated.

Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was provided free of cost from TATA-steel. Zinc acetate, sodium sulfide, congo red (CR), methyl orange (MO), rhodamine-B (RhB), crystal violet (CV), methylene blue (MB), and metanil yellow (MY), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich, India. For trapping study, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA), tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH), para-benzoquinone (p-BZQ) and were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as it is. The industrial wastewater samples (designed as WWS-1, WWS-2, WWS-3) were collected from textile industry, Sanganer, Jaipur.

ZnS embedded rGO nanosheet was prepared using precipitation methods (Sun et al., 2008). In brief, 50 mg rGO was taken in 40–50 mL DMF and sonicated for 20–30 min to disperse the material. After that, 60 mg zinc acetate was added to this solution followed by continuous stirring. Next, 500 µL of Na2S (0.62 M) was added dropwise in reaction mixture and stirring was continued for further 1 h. The solution was then centrifuged and water washed five to six times to extract extra reagents and residue was dried at ~60 °C to yield ZnS embedded rGO nanosheet was obtained.

The dyes RhB, CR, MY, MO, MB and CV were chosen as a model dye for the selectivity study to explore the photocatalytic characteristics of ZnS-rGO. For selectivity study, we used 20 mg/L concentration of all dyes with 0.5 mg/mL of ZnS-rGO. At first, solutions were stirred for 15 min and then placed under sunlight for next 120 min. For each 15-minute interval, a 1 mL aliquot was taken and centrifuged to remove the ZnS-rGO and clear solutions were used to take absorbance value at λmax at 501 nm for CR dye using UV–Vis spectroscopy. For kinetic study, CR dye at different concentration (20–100 mg/L) was used with a fixed concentration 0.5 mg/mL of ZnS-rGO and followed the same experiments. Next, effect of catalyst loading was performed by keeping constant concentration of dye 60 mg/L on addition of 0.1–0.7 mg/mL ZnS-rGO and repeat the same procedure. Some control experiments were performed using CR dye at 60 mg/L and 0.5 mg/mL ZnS-rGO using artificial bulb light and in dark condition. Another control experiment was done using rGO in place of ZnS-rGO catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evidence of radical formation and trapping study
ce:section.ce:para: Trapping study was performed using various scavengers. In brief, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA), para-benzoquinone (p-BZQ) and tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH) with 20 mM concentration were used for holes (h+), superoxide (O2 •–)and hydroxyl (OH•) scavengers, respectively (Singh et al., 2019). In this experiment, we used CR dye at 60 mg/L and 0.5 mg/mL ZnS-rGO and followed same experimental procedure. Next, aqueous solution of 0.05 mM NBT solution was prepared. Then, 5 mg ZnS-rGO was added to 10 mL NBT solution and kept under sunlight for further 2 h. The UV–vis spectra were recorded at different time interval.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis and recycle study
ce:section.ce:para: For real sample analysis, CR dye was spiked at 60 mg/L with industrial waste water and further treated with ZnS-rGO with concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. For recycle study, after the degradation of CR dye ZnS-rGO was centrifuged to separate from the whole solution and frequently rinsed with water, dried at 50 °C for use in photocatalytic degradation studies and repeated for 5 times.

In order to measure the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of ZnS-rGO, an ESCA+ Omicron Nanotechnology instrument was utilized. FEI Tecnai G2 F30 with a 300 kV operating voltage was used for high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Raman spectra were collected from a confocal Raman spectrometer at 532 nm. XRD study was performed using a Panalytical X Pert Pro instrument. Thermogravimetric (TGA) analysis was carried out at 25–750 °C and the heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in the presence of argon gas. The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected from Jasco V-730 spectrophotometer. The 1H NMR analysis was studied using a JEOL ECS-400 using DMSO‑d 6 solvent at 400 MHz. A BrukerVertex 70 FT-IR spectrophotometer was used to record FTIR spectra.

For this purpose, rGO has been collected from TATA steel and used for further modification. Scheme 1 shows the fabrication methodology of rGO with ZnS. For this purpose, zinc acetate and Na2S has been used as source of Zn and S respectively and in situ formed ZnS has been fabricated on rGO sheet. In order to explore the catalysts’ structure, TEM and HRTEM analysis of rGO and ZnS-rGO was done, Fig. 1 . The TEM image of rGO shows graphene sheet (Fig. 1a) and HRTEM image of rGO shows the graphitic fringes that have an inter-planar d-spacing value of 0.22 nm, Fig. 1b. (Ning et al., 2014) After fabrication of ZnS on rGO, ZnS nanoparticle has been observed on rGO surface (Fig. 1c) and HRTEM image of ZnS-rGO shows the ZnS nanoparticle with inter-planar d-spacing with 0.30 nm that relates to the graphitic plane of ZnS nanoparticle, Fig. 1d. (Lonkar et al., 2018) ZnS nanoparticles are marked as white circle in ZnS-rGO.

ZnS-rGO was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy to determine its elemental composition and the kind of chemical bonding it has. In Fig. 2 a, the full survey scan of XPS displays the typical peaks for the C1s, O1s, Zn2p, and S2p elements at ~283.5, ~531.5, ~1022.5, and ~168.5 eV respectively. (Bajpai et al., 2020; Bhati et al., 2018b; Tan et al., 2022; Tayyab et al., 2022) Fig. 2b, displays elemental composition in percentage of carbon ~67.8%, oxygen ~16.6%, zinc ~5.8% and sulphur ~9.8%. Spectrum of C1s with high resolution in Fig. 2c displays various binding at ~286.9, ~284.9 and ~284.3, eV corresponds to C-O/C=O, C–C, and C=C bonds, respectively (Bajpai et al., 2020; Garg et al., 2022a). Fig. 2d displays the deconvolution spectra of O1s and the binding sites for C=O and C-O are at ~532.1 and ~530.3 eV, respectively (Bhati et al., 2018b). The high-resolution spectrum of Zn2p in Fig. 2e shows the binding energies at ~1045.3 eV and ~1022 eV refers to the Zn2p1/2 and Zn2p3/2 respectively (Bhati et al., 2018b; Liu et al., 2021b). The deconvoluted S2p spectrum in Fig. 2f shows the peak at ~169.3, ~167.2, ~164.7, ~163, and ~161.7 eV refers to sulfate bonding, C-S-O, C–S=O, C=S, and Zn-S respectively (Baik et al., 2017; Karikalan et al., 2017; Kaushik et al., 2022b; Saini et al., 2020).

The comparative Raman spectra of rGO and ZnS-rGO has been shown in Fig. 3 a. D and G bands appear in the Raman spectra of rGO at ~1341 and ~1583 cm−1, respectively and indicates the sp3 and sp2 carbon framework in rGO (Mahvelati-Shamsabadi and Goharshadi, 2018). The integrated area of the D and G bands has used to compute the ID/IG ratio, is determine to be ~2.5. The ZnS-rGO Raman spectra exhibit the characteristic D and G band features of rGO, with additional peaks in the range of 300–500 cm−1, corresponds to ZnS. (Indhumathy and Prakasam, 2020) The integrated area of the D and G bands has used to compute the ID/IG ratio, is determine to be ~1.59. XRD study of the ZnS-rGO have been performed, Fig. 3b. The three peaks are observed 2θ at 28.83° (111), 48.53° (220), 57.33° (311) confirming the presence of ZnS is compared with the JCPDS of PDF card number 80–0020 and 65–9585 (Khan et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017). The thermal stability of ZnS-rGO has been explored by thermal analysis. The comparative TGA thermograms of rGO and ZnS-rGO are displayed in Supporting Information Fig. S1.; results show that ZnS-rGO lost only ~12 wt% mass at 750 °C, in comparison to rGO (~10 wt%) confirming high thermal stability of ZnS-rGO up to 750 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective photocatalytic degradation of congo red (CR) dye and optimization for maximum photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The sunlight -assisted degradation efficiency of ZnS-rGO has been explored using different model dyes RhB, CR, MY, MO, MB, CV at concentration of 20 mg/L, Supporting Information Fig. S2. Results conclude that CR dye is selectively photodegraded among tested six dyes. Primary amine group (–NH2) is present only in CR dye and –NH2 group interacts with –COO- group of ZnS-rGO through H-bonding. To confirm this, we have performed photocatalytic degradation study at different pH, Supporting Information Fig. S3. Results indicate that on moving to acidic pH (pH = 5), % of CR dye degradation increases significantly and further decreases at pH = 3. On moving from neutral to basic pH, the photocatalytic degradation of CR dye decreases gradually. The influence of pH on photocatalytic degradation can be explained by zeta potential. At pH = 5, the zeta potential value of ZnS-rGO is negative (-11 mV) and interaction with protonated NH2 of CR dye becomes more effective as –NH3 + having stronger H-bond donor character than –NH2. In contrast, at basic pH, the zeta potential value of ZnS-rGO becomes more negative to −25 mV and interaction becomes less effective with negatively charged CR dye. Thus, CR dye photocatalytic degradation is more effective at pH = 5.

At first, we have performed the photocatalytic degradation studies at different time like 10 am-12 pm, 12–2 pm, and 2–4 pm and results indicates that the best time is 12–2 pm for the degradation of CR dye, Supporting Information Fig. S4. Next, we have performed photocatalytic degradation of different concentration of CR dye solution (20–100 mg/L) keeping catalyst concentration constant at 0.5 mg/mL, Fig. 4 a. The plot of C/C0 versus time for different concentration of CR dye shows that 20–60 mg/L CR dye degraded efficiently and with increasing the dye concentration from 60 to 100 mg/L, degradation efficiency of CR dye decreases. Next, the obtained data from variable concentration studies was fitted with pseudo-first-order kinetics in order to determine the dye degradation rate using ZnS-rGO, Fig. 4b. Data is linearly fitted with good regression coefficient value (R2 > 0.97) to the pseudo-first-order kinetics and confirms that the pseudo-first-order kinetics are followed during the photocatalytic degradation of CR dye. The rate of photocatalytic degradation has been calculated using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model equation (Kaushik et al., 2022a).

The rate constant is the slope of the linearly fitted curve and is found to be 0.0266 min−1 for CR dye of 60 mg/L. The rate constant and half-life of photocatalytic degradation of CR dye against their variable concentration plotted, Fig. 4c. Results indicate that rate constant of photocatalytic degradation decreases and half-life of photocatalytic degradation increases with increasing the concentration of dye.

Next, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of ~60 mg/L CR dye under sunlight has been observed by changing the concentration of ZnS-rGO from 0.1 to 0.7 mg/mL, Fig. 4d. According to the findings, the dye removal efficiency increases with increasing the loading amount of ZnS-rGO up to 0.5 mg/mL as the active site of catalyst increases. After that the removal efficiency remain constant as 0.5 mg/mL ZnS-rGO is sufficient to degrade 60 mg/L of CR dye. Thus, we have chosen 0.5 mg/mL of ZnS-rGO photocatalyst to degrade 60 mg/L of CR dye.

The photocatalytic efficiency of ZnS-rGO has been investigated towards the degradation of CR (60 mg/L) dye under sunlight (SL), bulb light (BL) and in dark (D). Another control experiment, degradation of CR dye in absence of ZnS-rGO and in presence of only rGO has also been performed under sunlight. Plot of time-dependent relative concentration change (C/C0) has shown that CR dye has degraded in presence of ZnS-rGO catalyst under sunlight within ~120 min, Fig. 5 a. The degradation efficiency of CR dye under sunlight is 98% which is ~three times higher than under artificial bulb light condition, Fig. 5b. In contrast, degradation is not significant in dark and bulb light condition. Additionally, it is revealed that the proportion of CR dye degradation is insignificant in absence of ZnS-rGO and in presence of only rGO, Fig. 5b. Thus, we conclude that the ZnS-rGO is working as a photocatalyst under sunlight. Next, CR dye photocatalytic degradation follows the pseudo-first-order kinetics, which is confirmed by data that is linearly fitted to it with a reasonable regression coefficient value (R2 > 0.97), Fig. 5c. The half-life and rate constant of photocatalytic degradation of CR dye against under sunlight, artificial bulb light and dark is plotted, Fig. 5d. The obtained results indicate that rate constant of CR dye photocatalytic degradation is maximum under sunlight and is almost ~6 times higher than the rate of CR dye degradation under bulb light. Moreover, half-life of CR dye is maximum in dark condition, Fig. 5d.

A comparative literature review of earlier research demonstrating the percentage of CR dye degradation by photocatalysis, the source of light, concentration of CR dye, employing various photocatalysts has compiled in a table, demonstrating the significance of the current investigation, Supporting Information, Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of CR dye in presence of ZnS-rGO under sunlight
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, photocatalytic degradation occurs through major active species O2 •–, OH• and h+. To address the role of the active species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of CR dye in the presence of ZnS-rGO, we have used different well-known scavengers to trap different active species such as para-benzoquinone (p-BZQ) for superoxide radicals (O2 •–), t-BuOH for hydroxyl radicals (OH•), and Na2EDTA for holes (h+). (Liu et al., 2021a; Singh et al., 2019) The digital photographic images of 60 mg/L CR dye solution along with different scavengers, Fig. 6 a. Fig. 6b shows the images of CR dye solution after photocatalytic degradation in presence of different scavengers, suggesting that CR dye is not degraded in presence of p-benzoquinone (p-BZQ) scavengers. Next, percentage of CR dye degradation has plotted in bar diagram, indicates the degradation efficiency of CR dye decreases significantly in presence of p-benzoquinone (p-BZQ) with respect to control, suggesting the involvement of superoxide radical (O2 •–) to degrade CR dye, Fig. 6c. Additionally, the production of superoxide radical (O2 •–) from ZnS-rGO under sunlight has been confirmed using nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) test, Fig. 6d. (Rajendran et al., 2022) Results show that the absorbance at 259 nm decreases significantly within 2 h in presence of ZnS-rGO under sunlight, suggesting the production of superoxide radical (O2 •–).

The plausible schematic diagram of CR dye degradation using ZnS-rGO under sunlight has been illustrated in Scheme 2 . The electrons from ZnS are captured by the rGO framework after being raised from the ground state of ZnS to excited state in the presence of sunlight. because of the electrons' promotion from HOMO to LUMO, O2 •– radicals produce and utilized alone or in association with hydroxyl radicals, can cause the breakdown of the CR dye into smaller hydrocarbons (Kaushik et al., 2022a; Mitra et al., 2019).

To confirm the photocatalytic degradation of CR dye, we have performed 1H NMR study by collecting degraded product of CR dye with respect to control CR, Fig. 7 . CR dye's chemical structure is displayed in Fig. 7a and H-atoms are labelled. For NMR study, each of the samples are taken in DMSO‑d 6 solvent. Fig. 7b shows the 1H NMR spectra of CR dye with their characteristic peaks in the aromatic region ~7–9 ppm while these aromatic proton signals are absent in the 1H NMR spectra of degraded dye, suggesting the degradation of CR dye in presence ZnS-rGO under sunlight, Fig. 7c. In brief, δ (ppm) shifts at ~d (8.74 ppm, H-1), ~d (8.46 ppm, H-2), ~s (8.31, H-3), ~d (8.11 ppm, H-4), ~d (7.96 ppm, H-5), ~s (7.77, H-6), ~t (7.59 ppm, H-7), and ~t (7.50 ppm, H-8) has observed for CR dye and these peaks are not present in degraded dye (Pigorsch et al., 1994). Additionally, some new peaks in the aliphatic region have been observed δ (ppm) shifts at ~4.14, ~2.1, ~1.22, ~1.12 and ~1.10, suggesting the degradation of CR dye to small aliphatic hydrocarbon (Kaushik et al., 2022a; Saini et al., 2019). Additionally, a comparative FTIR analysis of CR and degraded product of CR was analyzed. The peak at ~1606 cm−1 corresponds to N=N bond, at ~1447 cm−1 for S=O stretching, at ~1225 cm−1 corresponds to C–N bond stretching, and at 1062 cm−1 for C-OH bond stretching disappear after photocatalytic degradation of CR dye using ZnS-rGO, Supporting Information Fig. S5. The new peaks at ~3235 cm−1 corresponds to O–H bond stretching, ~865 cm−1 corresponds to C=C bending, and other aliphatic hydrocarbon bending vibrations at lower wavenumber appears in degraded product of CR, indicating the breakdown of CR dye. Results conclude that structure of azo dye CR degrades in presence of ZnS-rGO under sunlight (Chandanshive et al., 2017; Umamaheswari et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CR dye degradation in different wastewater sample and recycle study
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the real-life application potential of ZnS-rGO on photocatalytic degradation of CR dye, we have performed degradation study by spiking CR dye (60 mg/L) in different wastewater samples (WWS-1, WWS-2 and WWS-3), Fig. 8 a. (Dalal et al., 2021) Photocatalytic degradation of CR dye spiked wastewater sample has performed in presence of 0.5 mg/mL ZnS-rGO under sunlight and UV-absorbance at 501 nm has estimated after 120 min. The dye removal % bar plot shows that CR dye in wastewater sample has degraded with 90% efficiency, suggesting the real field application potential of ZnS-rGO as a photocatalyst. Further to explore the potential for multicyclic use of ZnS-rGO as a catalyst, recycle study is performed by recollecting the ZnS-rGO after using as a catalyst followed by reusing for the CR dye photocatalytic degradation, Fig. 8b. It has been observed that dye removal efficiency decreases after five cycles and remains above 65% up to the five cycles. To examine the stability of the recovered sample after recyclability studies, we have performed TEM analysis. (Supporting Information Fig. S6) Result indicates the presence of ZnS nanoparticle on rGO surface even after recyclability studies, confirming the retained property of ZnS-rGO catalyst.

The ZnS nanoparticle has been embedded on the rGO surface using a straightforward co-precipitation approach. As it is created, ZnS-rGO is used as a photocatalyst to selectively degrade CR dye under sunlight with a high photocatalytic degradation efficiency of ~98%. The rate of reaction under renewable source sunlight is ~3 times faster than artificial bulb light. The effect of dye concentration and catalyst loading on the photocatalytic degradation of CR dye has also been explored. Kinetics studies confirm that the rate of photocatalytic degradation of dye follows pseudo-first-order kinetic models. It is also investigated that the superoxide radical is the main responsible for degrading CR dye under sunlight in the presence of our catalyst ZnS-rGO. The recycling study findings and dye degradation in real wastewater show that ZnS-rGO demonstrates remarkable photocatalytic effectiveness. These graphene-based effective photocatalysts could be used to aid in the cleanup of industrial effluent water containing organic dye contaminants.
